title: Fencibles

infobox.value: (from the word *defencible*) were military units raised in the [British Empire](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire") during the 18th and 19th centuries for the purpose of defending against foreign invasion during times of war. The first fencible units [were raised in Scotland](/wiki/Highland_Fencible_Corps "Highland Fencible Corps") in 1759 during the [Seven Years' War](/wiki/Seven_Years%27_War "Seven Years' War") as the [Scottish militia](/wiki/Militia_(Great_Britain) "Militia (Great Britain)") was not then active over concerns stemming from the [Jacobite risings](/wiki/Jacobite_risings "Jacobite risings"). In contrast to the militia, Fencible units were [regular troops](/wiki/Regular_troops "Regular troops") who could be stationed anywhere in the [British Isles](/wiki/British_Isles "British Isles") and whose officers were appointed by [the Crown](/wiki/The_Crown "The Crown"), though although like the militia were generally exempt from overseas service.

"Structured data on this page hosted by Wikidata [g]") Appearance move to sidebar hide From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia Type of historical British military unit For American units with fencibles in their name, see [Fencibles (American)](/wiki/Fencibles_(American) "Fencibles (American)"). For the New Zealand football team, see [Fencibles United](/wiki/Fencibles_United "Fencibles United"). [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a4/Francis_Macnab.jpg/250px-Francis_Macnab.jpg)](/wiki/File:Francis_Macnab.jpg) c. 1810 portrait of [Lieutenant-colonel](/wiki/Lieutenant_colonel_(United_Kingdom) "Lieutenant colonel (United Kingdom)") Francis Macnab of the [Breadalbane Fencibles](/wiki/Breadalbane_Fencible_Regiment "Breadalbane Fencible Regiment") **Fencibles** (from the word *defencible*) were military units raised in the [British Empire](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire") during the 18th and 19th centuries for the purpose of defending against foreign invasion during times of war. The first fencible units [were raised in Scotland](/wiki/Highland_Fencible_Corps "Highland Fencible Corps") in 1759 during the [Seven Years' War](/wiki/Seven_Years%27_War "Seven Years' War") as the [Scottish militia](/wiki/Militia_(Great_Britain) "Militia (Great Britain)") was not then active over concerns stemming from the [Jacobite risings](/wiki/Jacobite_risings "Jacobite risings"). In contrast to the militia, Fencible units were [regular troops](/wiki/Regular_troops "Regular troops") who could be stationed anywhere in the [British Isles](/wiki/British_Isles "British Isles") and whose officers were appointed by [the Crown](/wiki/The_Crown "The Crown"), though although like the militia were generally exempt from overseas service. Fencible units were again raised during the [American War of Independence](/wiki/American_War_of_Independence "American War of Independence") and the [French Revolutionary and Napoleonic Wars](/wiki/French_Revolutionary_and_Napoleonic_Wars "French Revolutionary and Napoleonic Wars"), playing a major role in the suppression of the [Irish Rebellion of 1798](/wiki/Irish_Rebellion_of_1798 "Irish Rebellion of 1798"). During the French Revolutionary and Napoleonic Wars, naval militia forces known as the [Sea Fencibles](/wiki/Sea_Fencibles "Sea Fencibles") and [River Fencibles](/wiki/River_Fencibles "River Fencibles") were raised for coastal defence duties.[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTE''National_Maritime_Museum''2011-1) Several fencible units were raised for service in [Canada](/wiki/Canadas "Canadas") and fought in the [War of 1812](/wiki/War_of_1812 "War of 1812"). ## History [[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fencibles&action=edit§ion=1 "Edit section: History")] [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d2/Charles_Johnstone_when_an_ensign_in_the_Hopetoun_Fencibles%2C_with_four_unknown_soldiers.jpg/250px-Charles_Johnstone_when_an_ensign_in_the_Hopetoun_Fencibles%2C_with_four_unknown_soldiers.jpg)](/wiki/File:Charles_Johnstone_when_an_ensign_in_the_Hopetoun_Fencibles,_with_four_unknown_soldiers.jpg) 1795 engraving of an ensign and four privates of the Hopetoun Fencibles The first regiments were raised in Scotland in 1759. In England [militia regiments](/wiki/Militia_regiment "Militia regiment") were raised for internal defence in the absence of the regular army; but it was not deemed prudent to extend the system to Scotland, the inhabitants of which, it was supposed, could not yet be safely entrusted with arms because of [The 'Fifteen'](/wiki/The_%27Fifteen%27 "The 'Fifteen'") and [The 'Forty-Five'](/wiki/The_%27Forty-Five%27 "The 'Forty-Five'") [rebellions](/wiki/Jacobite_risings "Jacobite risings"). Groundless as the reasons for this caution undoubtedly were in regard to the Lowlands, it would certainly have been hazardous at a time when [the Stuarts](/wiki/Jacobitism#Henry_IX "Jacobitism") and their adherents were still plotting a restoration to have armed [the clans](/wiki/Scottish_clan#Collapse_of_the_Clan_system "Scottish clan").[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrowne1854368-2) Unlike the militia regiments which were raised by ballot, the Fencibles were to be raised by the ordinary mode of recruiting, and like the regiments of the line, the officers were to be appointed, and their commissions signed by the king.[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrowne1854368-2) Most fencible regiments had no liability for overseas service however there were exceptions. Ireland while not united with the [Kingdom of Great Britain](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Great_Britain "Kingdom of Great Britain") until 1801 was the destination for several British fencible regiments during the [Rebellion of 1798](/wiki/Rebellion_of_1798 "Rebellion of 1798") where they fought in some pitched battles. The [3rd Argyllshire Regiment](/wiki/Argyle_Fencibles_(1798) "Argyle Fencibles (1798)"), who like some other fencible regiments had terms of service that extended to any part of Europe, garrisoned Gibraltar (as did [Banffshire Fencibles](/wiki/Banffshire_Fencibles "Banffshire Fencibles"), [2nd Argyllshire Fencibles](/wiki/Argyle_Fencibles_(1794) "Argyle Fencibles (1794)"), and the [Prince of Wales Own Fencibles](/wiki/Prince_of_Wales_Own_Fencibles "Prince of Wales Own Fencibles")[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMusteen2011218_(note_540)-3))[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrowne1854384-4)[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEScobie1914355-5) The [Dumbarton Fencibles Regiment](/wiki/Dumbarton_Fencibles "Dumbarton Fencibles") was raised in Scotland, garrisoned [Guernsey](/wiki/Guernsey "Guernsey"), fought in Ireland, and a detachment escorted prisoners to Prussia.[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEScobie1914356-6) The [Ancient Irish Fencibles](/wiki/Ancient_Irish_Fencibles "Ancient Irish Fencibles") were sent to Egypt where they took part in the operations against the French in 1801.[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEScobie1914362-7) Fencible regiments tended to be less effective than regular troops for military duties; with problems of lack of education and vulnerability to disease. In Ireland, fencibles would take part in inter-regimental brawls and attacks on regular army soldiers. Some regiments of fencibles, however, were noted for exceptional service.[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBartlettJeffery1997[[Category:Wikipedia_articles_needing_page_number_citations_from_August_2017]][[[Wikipedia:Citing_sources|page needed]]]-8) ## Timeline [[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fencibles&action=edit§ion=2 "Edit section: Timeline")] Further information: [List of British fencible regiments](/wiki/List_of_British_fencible_regiments "List of British fencible regiments") ### Highland Fencibles [[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fencibles&action=edit§ion=3 "Edit section: Highland Fencibles")] Main article: [Highland Fencible Corps](/wiki/Highland_Fencible_Corps "Highland Fencible Corps") The [Scottish Highlands supplied fencible regiments](/wiki/Highland_Fencible_Corps "Highland Fencible Corps") for most of the second half of the 18th century. The first regiment raised was the [Argyle Fencibles](/wiki/Argyle_Fencibles_(1759) "Argyle Fencibles (1759)") in 1759 and the last was the [MacLeod Fencibles](/wiki/MacLeod_Fencibles "MacLeod Fencibles") in 1779. In all over 20 regiments were created, although they were not all in existence at the same time. Some Highland fencibles regiments saw action in the [Irish Rebellion of 1798](/wiki/Irish_Rebellion_of_1798 "Irish Rebellion of 1798"), while other performed garrison and policing duties in Britain, Ireland, the Channel Islands and Gibraltar.[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrowne1854368–384-9) ### American Revolutionary War [[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fencibles&action=edit§ion=4 "Edit section: American Revolutionary War")] The [Royal Fencible Americans](/wiki/Royal_Fencible_Americans "Royal Fencible Americans") was a [Loyalist](/wiki/Loyalist_(American_Revolution) "Loyalist (American Revolution)") unit raised by the British in [Nova Scotia](/wiki/Nova_Scotia "Nova Scotia") in 1775, that successfully withstood an attack by [Patriot](/wiki/Patriot_(American_Revolution) "Patriot (American Revolution)") forces under [Jonathan Eddy](/wiki/Jonathan_Eddy "Jonathan Eddy") at the [Battle of Fort Cumberland](/wiki/Battle_of_Fort_Cumberland "Battle of Fort Cumberland").[[10]](#cite_note-10) ### Irish Rebellion of 1798 [[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fencibles&action=edit§ion=5 "Edit section: Irish Rebellion of 1798")] Fencibles were raised for the entirety of the [Irish Rebellion](/wiki/Irish_Rebellion_of_1798 "Irish Rebellion of 1798"). ### French Revolutionary and Napoleonic Wars [[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fencibles&action=edit§ion=6 "Edit section: French Revolutionary and Napoleonic Wars")] Fencible units were raised in the United Kingdom and [Isle of Man](/wiki/Isle_of_Man "Isle of Man") during the early years of the war. This included not only land regiments but also the [Sea Fencibles](/wiki/Sea_Fencibles "Sea Fencibles") (raised in 1798 and disbanded in 1810). By the [Peace of Amiens](/wiki/Peace_of_Amiens "Peace of Amiens") in 1802, all Fencible Regiments had been disbanded and those members willing to continue serving had been transferred to regular army regiments. When the [Napoleonic Wars](/wiki/Napoleonic_Wars "Napoleonic Wars") resumed the British used alternative methods to defend the [Home Nations](/wiki/Home_Nations "Home Nations") (see for example the [Additional Forces Acts 1803](/wiki/Additional_Forces_Acts_1803 "Additional Forces Acts 1803")) and with the exception of the [Royal Manx Fencibles](/wiki/List_of_British_fencible_regiments#Manx_fencibles "List of British fencible regiments") (third corps, 1803–1811) no more fencible regiments were raised for home defence. ### War of 1812 [[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fencibles&action=edit§ion=7 "Edit section: War of 1812")] See also: [List of British fencible regiments § Further information](/wiki/List_of_British_fencible_regiments#Further_information "List of British fencible regiments"), and [Canadian units of the War of 1812](/wiki/Canadian_units_of_the_War_of_1812 "Canadian units of the War of 1812") [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ea/Corporal%2C_Battalion_Company%2C_The_Canadian_Regiment_of_Fencible_Infantry%2C_c.1814.jpg/250px-Corporal%2C_Battalion_Company%2C_The_Canadian_Regiment_of_Fencible_Infantry%2C_c.1814.jpg)](/wiki/File:Corporal,_Battalion_Company,_The_Canadian_Regiment_of_Fencible_Infantry,_c.1814.jpg) Illustration of a [Canadian Regiment of Fencible Infantry](/wiki/Canadian_Regiment_of_Fencible_Infantry "Canadian Regiment of Fencible Infantry") corporal in 1814 In the early years of the 19th Century, regiments of Fencibles were raised in [the Canadas](/wiki/The_Canadas "The Canadas"), [New Brunswick](/wiki/New_Brunswick "New Brunswick"), [Newfoundland](/wiki/Newfoundland "Newfoundland"), and Nova Scotia. The regiments were liable for service in North America only (although the New Brunswick Fencibles volunteered for general service and became the [104th Regiment of Foot](/wiki/104th_(New_Brunswick)_Regiment_of_Foot "104th (New Brunswick) Regiment of Foot") in the regular army). All but one of these regiments saw action in the [War of 1812](/wiki/War_of_1812 "War of 1812"). The regiments were disbanded in 1816 and 1817, after the War of 1812. Although the units were disbanded, several regiments in Canada continue to perpetuate their historic lineage. Most of the Fencible regiments were formed in 1803, including the [Nova Scotia Fencibles](/wiki/Nova_Scotia_Fencibles "Nova Scotia Fencibles"), the [Canadian Regiment of Fencible Infantry](/wiki/Canadian_Regiment_of_Fencible_Infantry "Canadian Regiment of Fencible Infantry") (perpetuated by the [Royal 22nd Regiment](/wiki/Royal_22nd_Regiment "Royal 22nd Regiment")), the Royal Newfoundland Fencibles (perpetuated by the [Royal Newfoundland Regiment](/wiki/Royal_Newfoundland_Regiment "Royal Newfoundland Regiment")), and the New Brunswick Regiment of Fencible Infantry (perpetuated by the [Royal New Brunswick Regiment](/wiki/Royal_New_Brunswick_Regiment "Royal New Brunswick Regiment")). The [Glengarry Light Infantry Fencibles](/wiki/Glengarry_Light_Infantry "Glengarry Light Infantry") (perpetuated by the [Stormont, Dundas and Glengarry Highlanders](/wiki/Stormont,_Dundas_and_Glengarry_Highlanders "Stormont, Dundas and Glengarry Highlanders")) was formed in February 1812, several months prior to the war. In addition to these Canadian units, the Michigan Fencibles, a small unit of 45 troops, was raised at [Fort Mackinac](/wiki/Fort_Mackinac "Fort Mackinac") in 1813.[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChartrandEmbleton199841-11) ### Bombay Fencibles [[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fencibles&action=edit§ion=8 "Edit section: Bombay Fencibles")] The [Bombay Fencibles](/w/index.php?title=Bombay_Fencibles&action=edit&redlink=1 "Bombay Fencibles (page does not exist)") were raised in 1799 by the following order of the Bombay Army: "A regiment consisting of two battalions of natives to be, under the denomination of the 'Bombay fencibles,' raised from the inhabitants of Bombay, Salsette, and Caranjah, on condition of not being liable to serve out of the said bounds; at the \* pay to each private of 5 rupees 2 quarters per month, besides clothing: which last they will receive from the [honorable company](/wiki/East_India_Company "East India Company")".[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoor18016-12) The [5th battalion](/wiki/103rd_Mahratta_Light_Infantry "103rd Mahratta Light Infantry") of the [Maratha Light Infantry](/wiki/Maratha_Light_Infantry "Maratha Light Infantry") was raised in December 1800 from the Bombay Fencibles as the 1st Battalion, the 9th Regiment of Bombay Native Infantry. ### Malta [[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fencibles&action=edit§ion=9 "Edit section: Malta")] The [Royal Malta Fencible Regiment](/wiki/Royal_Malta_Fencible_Regiment "Royal Malta Fencible Regiment") was in existence from 1815 to 1861 when it became the [Royal Malta Fencible Artillery](/w/index.php?title=Royal_Malta_Fencible_Artillery&action=edit&redlink=1 "Royal Malta Fencible Artillery (page does not exist)") until 1881. The term 'Fencible' was dropped in 1889 and it became the [Royal Malta Artillery](/wiki/Royal_Malta_Artillery "Royal Malta Artillery").[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTE''Armed_Forces_of_Malta''2015a-13)[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEArmed_Forces_of_Malta2015b-14) ### New Zealand Wars [[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fencibles&action=edit§ion=10 "Edit section: New Zealand Wars")] [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/53/John_Jermyn_Symonds.jpg/250px-John_Jermyn_Symonds.jpg)](/wiki/File:John_Jermyn_Symonds.jpg) Captain John Jermyn Symonds, second in command of Fencibles at [Onehunga](/wiki/Onehunga "Onehunga"). In 1847 the [Royal New Zealand Fencible Corps](/wiki/Royal_New_Zealand_Fencible_Corps "Royal New Zealand Fencible Corps") were raised and sent to New Zealand for the defence of the early settlers there. They were settled in four new outlying villages around Auckland, (then the capital), at [Onehunga](/wiki/Onehunga "Onehunga"), [Otahuhu](/wiki/Otahuhu "Otahuhu"), [Panmure](/wiki/Panmure,_New_Zealand "Panmure, New Zealand"), and [Howick](/wiki/Howick,_New_Zealand "Howick, New Zealand"), the largest of the four. About 75 fencibles and their sons took part as militia in the 1860s [New Zealand Wars](/wiki/New_Zealand_Wars "New Zealand Wars").[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTE''Herald''2010-15)[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAlexanderGibsonLa_RocheWaiuku1997112-16) ## See also [[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fencibles&action=edit§ion=11 "Edit section: See also")] * [Army Reserve (United Kingdom)](/wiki/Army_Reserve_(United_Kingdom) "Army Reserve (United Kingdom)"), the modern-day equivalent * [British Volunteer Corps](/wiki/British_Volunteer_Corps "British Volunteer Corps") * Canadian forces [Primary Reserve](/wiki/Primary_Reserve "Primary Reserve") * [History of the British Army](/wiki/History_of_the_British_Army "History of the British Army") * [Home Guard (United Kingdom)](/wiki/Home_Guard_(United_Kingdom) "Home Guard (United Kingdom)") * [Militia (British Dominions and Crown Colonies)](/wiki/Militia_(British_Dominions_and_Crown_Colonies) "Militia (British Dominions and Crown Colonies)") * [Militia (Great Britain)](/wiki/Militia_(Great_Britain) "Militia (Great Britain)") * [Militia (United Kingdom)](/wiki/Militia_(United_Kingdom) "Militia (United Kingdom)") * [Territorial Army](/wiki/Territorial_Army_(United_Kingdom) "Territorial Army (United Kingdom)") * [Yeomanry](/wiki/Yeomanry "Yeomanry") ## Notes 1997](#CITEREFAlexanderGibsonLa_RocheWaiuku1997), p. 112. ## References [[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fencibles&action=edit§ion=13 "Edit section: References")] * Alexander, R.; Gibson, G.; La Roche, A.;
